channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Stranded Ships Hoping for Escape Find a Turbulent Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Shipping companies had hoped to get their vessels out after this week’s U.S.-Iran deal but faced uncertainty on Friday as violence flared again in the region.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels at the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman, on Thursday.

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Says He Dislikes Deal With U.S., but Allowed It
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei distanced himself from the agreement, putting responsibility for it on Iran’s president, and said it does not mean acceding to U.S. demands.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Photos of Mojtaba Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader, are held up during a memorial for victims of the war, in Tehran in May.

item.title: For India, the Return of Iranian Oil Cannot Come Soon Enough
item.description: After months of shortages and uncertainty, India sees an opportunity to restore a once-crucial energy relationship.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.media:credit: Abhishek Chinnappa/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Mangalore Refinery in India. As recently as 2009, Iranian oil accounted for 14 percent of India’s crude imports, making Iran its second-largest supplier.

item.title: Inside Hegseth’s War on Diversity and Blocked Promotions of Women and Black Officers
item.description: A Black admiral fixed one of the Navy’s worst messes. Mr. Hegseth blocked his promotion anyway.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Kate Kelly
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has blocked the promotions of at least 40 senior officers to general and admiral ranks this year. About half of those are women or members of minority groups.

item.title: Trump’s Intelligence Pick Renews Debate: Is the Director of National Intelligence Needed?
item.description: The director of national intelligence’s office was created in the aftermath of the Sept. 11 attacks. Now some lawmakers want to shrink it.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard, seen at a hearing in March, is stepping down as the director of the office of national intelligence, a role that critics say has become too sprawling.

item.title: During Trump’s Immigration Crackdown, Minnesota Protester Remained Shackled in Hospital for Days
item.description: The story of one progressive activist arrested in Minnesota in January shows what critics say is the aggressive nature of the Trump administration’s response to those who have protested its immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Johnson, a longtime progressive activist, in the van where he alleges that federal agents assaulted him in January before leaving him shackled to a hospital bed for days before he was charged.

item.title: Burnham’s Stunning Victory in Makerfield Election May Trigger Starmer’s Last Stand
item.description: Andy Burnham, a Labour mayor, won a resounding majority in a special election on Thursday, making him eligible to challenge Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the country’s leadership.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Temilade Adelaja/Reuters
item.media:description: Andy Burnham speaking to his supporters on Friday after winning a seat in Parliament with almost 55 percent of the vote.

item.title: Who Is Andy Burnham, the Man Who Could Be Britain’s Next Prime Minister?
item.description: Charismatic, northern and exuding a relaxed optimism, Mr. Burnham is a contrast to Keir Starmer. His allies hope he could mend Labour’s relationship with voters.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Jon Super/Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, Labour’s most popular politician, arriving on Friday for the results announcement for the Makerfield by-election, in which he beat the populist right-wing Reform U.K. party.

item.title: ‘Game Changer’? Too Soon to Tell. But Ukraine Flexed in Striking Moscow.
item.description: The drone attack that sent plumes of smoke rising over Moscow intensified Ukrainian hopes of bringing the war to Russia.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady
item.media:credit: via Reuters
item.media:description: Smoke rising from an oil refinery in Moscow after a Ukrainian drone attack on Thursday, in an image obtained from social media.

item.title: A Missing Ingredient in Ukraine’s Barrages on Russia: Ballistic Missiles
item.description: Ukrainian drone attacks have achieved breakthroughs, but only ballistic missiles can push Russia to reconsider its war objectives, military experts and officials say.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:credit: via Reuters
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Moscow after a Ukrainian drone attack on Thursday, in an image from social media.

item.title: Back After Vanishing for 2 Weeks, Russian Banker Tries to Project Calm
item.description: The central bank leader Elvira S. Nabiullina reappeared for a news conference after a gap that highlighted deep tensions in the country’s political and economic elite.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Russian Central Bank Press Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Elvira S. Nabiullina, the governor of Russia’s central bank, spoke on Friday. The bank’s press office issued this photograph of the occasion.

item.title: David Thomson Loves the Movies but Not What They Have Done to America
item.description: How the film writer David Thomson found himself in a lover’s quarrel with cinema — and America.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott
item.category: A Sudden Flicker of Light: A Revisionist History of Movies (Book)
The New Biographical Dictionary of Film (Book)
item.media:credit: Nicholas Albrecht for The New York Times
item.media:description: Under the spell of the movies, David Thomson says, “we let the lifelike distract us from life.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘Regime Change,’ by Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.description: In “Regime Change,” two New York Times journalists offer a riveting chronicle of the weird fusion of reality and show business in the White House.
item.dc:creator: Fintan O’Toole
item.category: Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump (Book)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump addressing reporters before boarding Air Force One earlier this year.

item.title: Our Allies Are Wondering Whether Supporting the American War Machine Is Worth It
item.description: Is the Iran war an early sign of “superpower suicide"?
item.dc:creator: Linda Kinstler
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Hannah Pingree and Bobby Charles Will Face Off in Maine Governor’s Race
item.description: Mr. Charles, a conservative Republican, had led the field ahead of a ranked-choice runoff. But Ms. Pingree, a former Maine House speaker, had been in a tight race with other Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell

item.title: The (Very) Manhattan Moms Whose Sons Are Running for Congress
item.description: In a high-profile Democratic House primary, there’s a fair chance that would-be voters may have been sweet-talked by the proud mothers of leading candidates.
item.dc:creator: Matt Flegenheimer
item.media:credit: Heather Khalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lori Bores can be often seen wearing a sash identifying her as the mother of Alex Bores, a congressional candidate in Manhattan.

item.title: James Talarico Attacks Ken Paxton Over Child Abuse Case
item.description: The case, which resulted in a short jail sentence despite years of alleged abuse, has drawn outcry in Texas. So have the attempts to politicize it.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for Senate in Texas, held a news conference to call on Ken Paxton to release files related to Adam Hoffman in Waco, Texas, on Thursday.

item.title: Matt Dunlap Wins Democratic House Primary in Maine’s Swing District
item.description: Matt Dunlap defeated a candidate backed by the House Democratic campaign arm and is set to face former Gov. Paul LePage, a Republican, in a competitive general election.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Graeme Sloan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Matt Dunlap, the state auditor, was Maine’s secretary of state twice.

item.title: San Francisco Film Patrons Are Found Dead on Side of Highway
item.description: Judith Sheldon, 84, daughter of an Oscar-winning director, and her husband, Wylie, 86, were traveling to Oregon on a hot day. Their deaths are under investigation.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight and Madaleine Rubin

item.title: New York’s Dive Bars Have a Rich Past. Do They Have a Future?
item.description: Dive bars face an uncertain future with rising costs and changing social patterns.
item.dc:creator: Anna Kodé and Brian Karlsson
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times

item.title: Joan Cusack, the Actress Behind Jessie in ‘Toy Story,’ Takes the Lead
item.description: After 27 years, the beloved cowgirl doll voiced by Cusack is finally in charge, but the actress played an important role in shaping the character early on.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Arsenii Vaselenko for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cusack stepped away from acting to be a parent: “I wanted to be around and raise my kids, and if you’re acting, you always have to go away to work.”

item.title: ‘Toy Story 5’ Review: The Machines Have Come for the Children
item.description: In this latest installment of the popular franchise, Woody and Buzz Lightyear are back and must contend with Bonnie’s mesmerizing new toy.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Trump Put His Own Interests Above All in the Iran Deal
item.description: The Iran deal wasn’t about peace but answering Trump’s personal needs.
item.dc:creator: Thomas L. Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times. Source photographs: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images; Reuters

item.title: I Keep Telling People We’re Living in This Dystopian Novel
item.description: The 2010 novel “Super Sad True Love Story” foreshadowed our current world. So I asked its author, Gary Shteyngart, how he sees the world today and how we might find pleasure in it.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Annie Galvin
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Strikes in Moscow
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: via Reuters; Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Are You a Recent College Graduate Who Moved Back Home? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: If you lived with your parents for nine months or more after graduation, please tell us how you came to the decision and whether the job market influenced your thinking.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images

item.title: How the Nashville Zoo Became the Latest Face of Data Center Opposition
item.description: Outcry over plans to build a large data center next to the zoo has energized support for new restrictions in Nashville.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Seth Herald for The New York Times
item.media:description: The zoo has operated at its location in South Nashville since 1997.
